name: Haily Merritt
affiliation: Network Science Institute, Northeastern University London
abstract: Network approaches to neuroscience have traditionally sought universal organizational principles, but recent years have seen a shift to characterizing variability across individuals. By treating brain networks as phenotypes constrained by (epi)genetics and shaped by the environment, we can better understand how adaptive brain organization depends on context.
bio: Haily is a Postdoctoral Researcher at the Network Science Institute at Northeastern University London. She holds dual PhDs in Informatics and Cognitive Science from Indiana University.
themeRelevance: Approaching brain networks as phenotypic structures constrained by genetic and environmental contexts.

name: Phileas Dazeley-Gaist
topic: Tracking the Micro-Dynamics of Cultural Evolution; a Sympoietic Art Organism
abstract: We introduce the “Sympoietic Art Organism”, an experimental design to richly capture the bottom-up emergence of shared cultural heritage in a small community. Over three months, ten artists built a shared creative commons, resulting in a dataset of 411 original artworks and a citation network of 725 verified relationships between them. By modeling the network's growth as a time series, we provide a scalable ethnographic tool for investigating the mechanisms of cultural inheritance.
bio: Phileas is a human ecologist, creative technologist, and developer at Wolfram Research, focusing on human ecology, agent-based modeling, and digital humanities.
themeRelevance: Constructing and mapping citation networks of artistic creations to track cultural evolution in real time.

name: Katie Spoon
topic: Your doctor visits as a network: Discovering high-risk health trajectories
abstract: Most population health studies estimate the short-term effect of a single factor, leaving a gap in understanding long-term health trajectories at scale. Using Electronic Health Records for 8 million U.S. patients, we turn 90 million primary-care visits into a state-transition graph of health trajectories. Applying graph-based representation learning to this network allows us to identify where health trajectories diverge and which demographic groups follow specific paths.
themeRelevance: Reframing longitudinal health trajectories and clinical visit sequences as paths on a state-transition network.

name: Yessica Herrera
topic: The Art World as a Network
abstract: What actually makes an artist succeed? Talent is only part of it. Much of success and prestige travels through an invisible network of museums, art collectors, and preferential collaborations. We reveal how novel computational tools and AI can help discover network data to expose the mechanisms of prestige shaping the art world, and explore using network patterns to create art.
bio: Yessica is a scientist blending social complexity with behavioral research, network science, and data visualization to explore how art, science, and technology shape human creativity.
themeRelevance: Analyzing the art world as a complex network of museum curation, collector prestige, and creative collaborations.

name: Arhan Vora
topic: Sailing Through Phenotype Space: Discretizing Evolution as Population Dynamics on a Network of Phenotypes
abstract: Traditional evolutionary dynamics are modeled on continuous fitness landscapes. In this work, we take a network-centric approach by discretizing evolution as population dynamics on a network of phenotypes. We explore how structural properties of the phenotype network constrain evolutionary trajectories and adaptation to selective pressures, revealing how complex genetic patterns map onto network-based transitions.
themeRelevance: Discretizing biological evolutionary processes as population dynamics over networks of phenotypes.

name: Dr. Divya Sindhu Lekha
affiliation: Indian Institute of Information Technology Kottayam
topic: Winning at Chess Without Search: Predicting Game Outcomes from Evolving Interaction Networks
abstract: We model chess games as dynamic interaction networks, where pieces and positions form an evolving graph shaped by strategic decisions. By applying graph neural networks to these game trajectories, we can predict game outcomes (win/loss) well before the game concludes. This network-based approach demonstrates how competitive, sequential decision-making can be reframed to capture strategic signals without relying on deep lookahead.
bio: Divya is a researcher and engineer working at the intersection of network science, graphs, and graph machine learning to uncover patterns in complex systems.
themeRelevance: Treating strategic sequential moves in chess as an evolving piece-interaction network to predict outcomes.

name: Alejandra Ortiz Durán
abstract: We explore pattern formation and dynamical processes on complex network topologies by simulating reaction-diffusion systems directly on graphs. We investigate how topological features such as community structures, hub nodes, and path lengths govern spatial pattern emergence, with applications to biological systems and information flow.
bio: Alejandra is an applied mathematician and Computational Graphs & Geometry Developer at Wolfram. She holds a master's degree in Mathematics from UNAM and is currently pursuing a Ph.D. in Mathematical Biology.
themeRelevance: Developing mathematical formulations to study reaction-diffusion patterns and dynamics on complex graph structures.

name: Atiyab Zafar
affiliation: Department of Physics and Astrophysics, University of Delhi
topic: How Networks Think: Structure, Memory, and Collective Problem Solving
abstract: Individuals and organizations often solve complex problems with incomplete information and through interaction with others. We study an agent-based model in which agents face a shared boolean satisfiability problem, learn information through observation and communication, and update beliefs through simple local greedy repairs. Because behavior is otherwise homogeneous, differences in collective performance come from network structure: we show interesting behavior that emerges from the way networks are shaped, ranging from information traps and collective memory to broader patterns of coordination, learning, and adaptation.
bio: Atiyab is a researcher who works in the field of network science and complex systems. His research lies in the domain of evolving networks. He holds an M.Sc. in physics and is currently pursuing PhD in physics from Delhi University.
themeRelevance: Investigating how network topology influences collective problem-solving and information sharing in agent-based models.
